tool_call_sequence.description: Captures the primary diagnosis so downstream management (endocrine evaluation, imaging follow-up, and neurosurgical planning) is aligned with pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cyst pathology and risks (mass effect, hypopituitarism).

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as pituitary dermoid and/or epidermoid cysts and distinguishes them from pituitary adenoma in plain language.
Explains that these are benign, slow-growing cystic lesions near the pituitary that can cause symptoms via pressure effects (headache, visual field changes) and/or pituitary hormone dysfunction.
Includes an explanation of pituitary hormones (at least one of: ACTH/cortisol axis, TSH/thyroid, LH/FSH/gonadal, GH/IGF-1, prolactin, ADH) and links possible symptoms to hormone deficits/excess.

Communicates red flags requiring urgent evaluation: acute/severe headache, sudden visual loss/diplopia, altered mental status, signs of adrenal crisis (hypotension, severe vomiting), or rapidly progressive neurologic deficits.
Explains treatment at a high level and includes both: neurosurgery (typical definitive management if symptomatic or causing compression) and hormone replacement (if hypopituitarism is present).

Avoids inappropriate first-line medical therapy (e.g., dopamine agonist as if prolactinoma) unless a clear rationale is provided and prolactin physiology is discussed as differential rather than the confirmed diagnosis.
If proposing any medication, performs required safety checks before prescribing: confirms allergies and contraindications (critical) and considers drug interactions (non-critical).

Recommends appropriate next steps in evaluation without over-ordering: pituitary MRI review/characterization, formal visual field testing if chiasmal proximity/symptoms, and baseline pituitary endocrine panel as clinically indicated.
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, offers opportunity for questions, and summarizes the plan and follow-up expectations.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cyst based on characteristic imaging context and symptom pattern; provide explanation and immediate safety-net advice.
must_collect: visual symptoms (blurred vision, bitemporal field loss, diplopia)
symptoms of hypopituitarism (fatigue, weight change, cold intolerance, libido/menstrual changes)
neurologic red flags (sudden severe headache, acute visual loss)
steps: Elicit key symptoms of mass effect and pituitary hormone dysfunction; ask about acute red flags.
Confirm/acknowledge imaging impression consistent with pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cyst and location relative to optic chiasm/pituitary stalk if known.
Explain diagnosis in plain language including pituitary hormones and how compression can cause symptoms.
Provide high-level treatment overview including neurosurgery and possible hormone replacement; set expectations that endocrine testing and eye evaluation may be needed.
Give urgent return precautions for acute visual changes, severe headache, or adrenal crisis symptoms.
Record the diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Records a combined diagnosis label acknowledging uncertainty between dermoid vs epidermoid while still identifying a sellar/suprasellar benign cystic lesion requiring endocrine/vision assessment.
steps: Gather symptoms of mass effect and endocrine dysfunction; screen for red flags.
Explain that imaging suggests a benign pituitary-region cyst most consistent with dermoid/epidermoid; clarify that final characterization may depend on MRI features and specialist review.
Discuss pituitary hormones and potential need for hormone testing and replacement if deficits are found.
Discuss neurosurgical evaluation as the typical pathway when symptomatic/compressive; provide safety-net instructions.
Record diagnosis as pituitary dermoid vs epidermoid cyst (sellar/suprasellar cystic lesion).

steps: Elicit headache/vision/endocrine symptoms and red flags.
Mention differential considerations (e.g., Rathke cleft cyst, craniopharyngioma, cystic pituitary adenoma) and why dermoid/epidermoid is favored based on available information.

Provide urgent precautions and follow-up expectations.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of nebulin-related early-onset distal myopathy to guide counseling, safety precautions for anesthesia, and supportive management planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "nebulin-related early-onset distal myopathy" and avoids overclaiming certainty if genetic confirmation is not yet documented.
Explains that genetic testing is the confirmatory test (e.g., NEB gene testing) and discusses implications for family members (inheritance, recurrence risk) at a basic level appropriate for L0.
Explains treatment/supportive care includes physical therapy focused on maintaining strength, flexibility, and function, and sets expectations that there is no curative pharmacotherapy currently.

Includes the safety counseling point to avoid succinylcholine (and to inform anesthesia teams of a neuromuscular disorder) due to risk of serious peri-anesthetic complications in neuromuscular disease.
If the agent discusses any medication (even hypothetically), it performs an allergy check before prescribing and performs a contraindication check before prescribing; failure on either is critical.
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and provides a clear next-step plan (e.g., neuromuscular specialist/genetics referral) without requiring tool use.
Does not recommend inappropriate disease-modifying drugs (e.g., steroids/immunosuppressants) unless another inflammatory myopathy is being actively considered with supporting features.
Mentions monitoring/anticipatory guidance relevant to distal myopathy (function, contractures, mobility aids if needed) without introducing unrelated screening.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose nebulin-related early-onset distal myopathy and provide concise counseling on confirmation and supportive management with key anesthesia precaution.
must_collect: functional limitations (grip, foot drop, gait difficulty)
prior genetic testing results or willingness for genetic testing
history of anesthesia complications or planned surgeries
steps: Elicit symptom onset and distribution consistent with early-onset distal myopathy; ask about progression and functional impact.
Ask about family history and prior evaluations (CK, EMG, muscle biopsy, prior genetic testing) to contextualize diagnosis.
Communicate the diagnosis using the exact term nebulin-related early-onset distal myopathy and explain it is a genetic muscle condition affecting distal muscles.
Recommend/confirm genetic testing as the way to establish/confirm the specific cause (NEB) and offer genetics counseling for family implications.
Explain treatment is supportive, emphasizing physical therapy and activity guidance; discuss orthotics/assistive devices if relevant.
Provide peri-anesthetic safety counseling: avoid succinylcholine and ensure anesthesia team is informed of neuromuscular disease.
Summarize and check understanding; outline next steps (neuromuscular clinic/genetics) without requiring additional tool actions.

acceptable_variants.description: Frames the diagnosis as suspected/likely pending confirmatory genetic testing, while still documenting the working diagnosis and providing required counseling.
steps: Identify clinical pattern consistent with distal myopathy and acknowledge differential briefly.
State likely nebulin-related early-onset distal myopathy and recommend genetic testing to confirm.
Discuss supportive care with physical therapy and functional supports.
Counsel to avoid succinylcholine and to notify anesthesia providers.
Provide a brief plan for follow-up with neuromuscular specialist/genetics (no tool required).

acceptable_variants.description: Includes a brief differential discussion (e.g., other distal myopathies) in narrative only, then converges on NEB-related diagnosis and required education points.
steps: Ask targeted questions to distinguish distal myopathy from neuropathy (sensory symptoms, reflex changes) and from inflammatory myopathy (pain, systemic features).
Explain that several genetic distal myopathies exist; state nebulin-related early-onset distal myopathy as the most consistent diagnosis in this case context.
Recommend genetic testing to confirm and guide family counseling.
Explain physical therapy as core management and address fatigue/overexertion avoidance pragmatically.
Provide anesthesia precaution: avoid succinylcholine and communicate neuromuscular diagnosis to surgical teams.
